Figure 3: HQ vs RTP support for each question’s overall winner.





The Alignment Index itself is unremarkable this week — 0.73, basically flat versus last time, with the cumulative number at 0.81. But look at the flip count instead of the correlation: 3 of this poll’s 5 questions — Cinnamon, Cookie, Tart/Torte — saw HQ and RTP prefer genuinely different desserts. That’s a majority of this week’s questions, the most we’ve seen split by office in a single poll, and it brings the running tally to 7 flips since RTP joined. Still nothing here clears statistical significance on its own (these are small offices, and small offices are noisy) — but a correlation coefficient can look perfectly fine while more than half the individual questions quietly disagree underneath it, which is exactly what’s happening this week.

The Alignment Index bounces back a bit this week to 0.65 — still below Earth Day’s opening 0.90, but a recovery from last week’s dip — with the cumulative number at 0.85. Creamy produced the widest single-question office gap we’ve seen yet, 20% — both offices preferred Mousse, RTP just felt more strongly about it.
The more interesting story is in Fruity. RTP’s votes actually crowned a different winner than the whiteboard as a whole: while the combined total favored Pie, RTP alone preferred Cobbler. It’s not statistically significant with these sample sizes, but it’s the first time in the RTP era that the two offices would have crowned different desserts if they’d each voted alone.

title: Earth Day
text:
Welcome to our first poll including results from the RTP campus! Previously, our poll was collecting data from but ONE geographic region. Thankfully, we have been able to expand our reach to the EPA RTP campus, both diversifying our pool of respondents and upping our N. We can now include an exploration of geographic differences in our polling results where we find points of interest.








Figure 1: Votes and shares for each question (faceted).






How decisive were these results, combined?
TL;DR: Hiking crushed Picnicking (59–13, 82%) — the strongest signal of the week by far. Volunteering was the closest thing to a coin flip: Trash Pickup edged Invasive Species Removal by just 2 vote(s), 37–35.

Does HQ agree with RTP?
Now for the fun part: with two offices voting, we can finally ask whether HQ and RTP actually see things the same way, instead of just assuming it. For each question we run a two-proportion test comparing the two offices’ support for the overall winning option.

14%

Figure 3: HQ vs RTP support for each question’s overall winner.





None of the four gaps clear statistical significance (all p > 0.2) — with samples this size we shouldn’t expect them to. But two results stand out:

Enjoying Nature is where the offices agree almost perfectly: HQ went 82% for Hiking and RTP went 82% — a gap of well under a point.
Volunteering is the sneaky one. The combined total above looked like a coin flip, but that’s actually two offices confidently disagreeing and canceling each other out: HQ leaned toward Invasive Species Removal while RTP leaned toward Trash Pickup, the widest office gap of the day. A “toss-up” in the combined numbers can quietly be hiding a real split underneath.

Since this is the first week with two offices, let’s define a running HQ–RTP Alignment Index: the correlation between HQ’s and RTP’s vote shares across every option this week. Right now it’s 0.9 — pretty well aligned, Volunteering’s split aside. We’ll track this number as more polls come in from both offices.

Figure 2: Leader shares with 95% confidence intervals.







Do the crowd-avoiders and the strollers overlap?
“I’d rather come before or after peak bloom” and “I’d rather walk than picnic” both sound like the same peaceful, less-crowded kind of cherry blossom outing. Let’s bound the overlap.
Out of 35 overlapping voters, the “peaceful stroller” contingent could be as small as 20 or as large as 28 people (57%–80%), versus roughly 22 (63%) expected if the two preferences were unrelated. Since both options are already crowd favorites on their own, the floor stays high — there’s a good chance most of our peers just want a quiet walk among the blossoms, crowds be damned.

Figure 2: Leader shares with 95% confidence intervals.







Do the spice-and-mess people cluster together?
Runny yolks are messier, and hot sauce is a mess-tolerant condiment — so do the same people gravitate toward both? We didn’t ask people to link their two answers, but Yolk and Condiment happened to draw the exact same number of respondents (24 each), so the overlap bound is unusually tight this week.
Somewhere between 11 and 17 of the 24 overlapping voters (46%–71%) are both Runny Yolk and Hot Sauce people, versus about 13 (53%) expected if yolk texture and condiment choice are unrelated. Since both options are individually so popular, the floor on their overlap is high no matter what — the “messy egg” coalition is large by construction.

title: Toilet Paper Rolls: Over or Under
text:
Quick Look
Over: 21 votes (84.0%) Under: 4 votes (16.0%) Margin: 17 votes (≈ 68.0% points)








Figure 1: Counts and shares.

title: Toilet Paper Rolls: Over or Under
text:
Basic Stats
Is this different from a 50/50 split? Yes. Exact binomial test p‑value = 9.11^{-4}.
“Over” share 95% CI: [0.64, 0.95].
How many voters would need to switch to change the winner? Because each switch changes the margin by 2, you’d need 9 people to switch from Over to Under to flip the result. A perfect tie isn’t possible with 25 voters.

title: Toilet Paper Rolls: Over or Under

title: Cats vs. Dogs
text:
Figure 1: Votes and shares for Cats vs Dogs.





Dogs share: 66.7%, Cats share: 33.3%. Vote margin (Dogs − Cats): 14 votes = 33.3%.



Testing Cats proportion p against 0.5 using an exact binomial test.



    Exact binomial test

data:  14 and N
number of successes = 14, number of trials = 42, p-value = 0.04356
alternative hypothesis: true probability of success is not equal to 0.5
95 percent confidence interval:
 0.1956680 0.4954875
sample estimates:
probability of success 
             0.3333333 


Two‑sided p‑value: 0.0436 95% exact CI for p(Cats): 0.196 to 0.495
Express the Dogs lead as a margin in share terms, margin = p(Dogs) − p(Cats) = 1 − 2p(Cats). The corresponding 95% CI:


[1] 0.608663904 0.009025071
attr(,"conf.level")
[1] 0.95


Even the lower bound of the margin is positive, indicating a clear preference for Dogs.
How many flips would change the winner?
To tie, you’d need 7 votes to flip (7 Dogs → Cats). To give Cats a lead, you’d need 8 flips (8 votes).



Under p = 0.5 with N = 42, the probability to be at least this far from 50/50 (two‑sided) is the binomial test p‑value.
We can also simulate the Cats counts under 50/50:








Figure 2: Simulated Cats counts under a 50/50 world (10,000 polls, N = 42). The observed 14 is marked.





Fraction of simulations at least as extreme (two‑sided):


[1] 0.0464





Binomial likelihood for p(Cats) given 14 out of 42 votes:








Figure 3: Binomial likelihood L(p) for p(Cats) given 14/42 (scaled to max = 1).





With a flat prior Beta(1,1), the posterior is Beta(15,29). Credible interval and the probability Cats < 0.5 (i.e., Dogs lead):








Figure 4: Posterior for p(Cats) with Beta(1,1) prior (posterior = Beta(15,29)).





95% credible interval for p(Cats): [0.210, 0.485]
Posterior probability Dogs lead (p(Cats) < 0.5): 0.9842
Posterior predictive for a repeat poll of size 42: chance Cats win, tie, or Dogs win.


pp_cat_win     pp_tie pp_dog_win 
    0.0517     0.0270     0.9213 


Bayes factor for “not 50/50” vs “p = 0.5” using Jeffreys prior Beta(0.5,0.5).


[1] 1.319843


BF10 > 1 supports “not 50/50”; values around 3–10 are often called “moderate” evidence.





risk_difference      risk_ratio       odds_dogs        cohens_h 
      0.3333333       2.0000000       2.0000000       0.6796738 


Dogs lead by 33.3% in share. Dogs have about 2:1 the share of Cats and odds about 2:1. Cohen’s h ≈ 0.68 (medium‑to‑large difference; 0.2 small, 0.5 medium, 0.8 large).











Figure 5: Waffle chart: 42 voters, Cats = 14, Dogs = 28.

title: Hello there!
text: This site hosts the results from and ruminations on The Hallway Poll. The Hallway Poll was developed to help bring joy and intrigue to the WJCS 4232 suite and all those who pass through it. Please explore the site by using the navigation bar to explore.

title: Cheesy Snacks
text:
Vote counts and shares








Figure 1: Votes and shares for each snack.

title: Cheesy Snacks
section: Inference: does anyone lead in the poll?
text:
Inference: does anyone lead in the poll?
The natural null here is p = 0.5 for Cheez-Its, with N = 42



    Exact binomial test

data:  21 and 42
number of successes = 21, number of trials = 42, p-value = 1
alternative hypothesis: true probability of success is not equal to 0.5
95 percent confidence interval:
 0.3419476 0.6580524
sample estimates:
probability of success 
                   0.5 


The p-value is 1, a perfect tie gives no evidence either way. The 95% exact CI for the Cheez-Its share p is:


[1] 0.3419476 0.6580524
attr(,"conf.level")
[1] 0.95


We can also express uncertainty for the vote-margin (Cheez-Its minus Goldfish share). Share margin = 2p - 1, the 95% CI for the margin is just 2 x CI(p) - 1:


[1] -0.3161048  0.3161048
attr(,"conf.level")
[1] 0.95

title: Cheesy Snacks
section: Probability of a tie if it really is a fair 50/50 world of Cheez-Its and Goldfish
text:
Probability of a tie if it really is a fair 50/50 world of Cheez-Its and Goldfish
If each of the 42 voters is a fair coin flip, the probability of a tie is:


[1] 0.1223857


That’s about 12.2%. Ties are not rare with samples this small.

title: Cheesy Snacks
section: What margins should we expect with N = 42 at p = 0.5
text:
What margins should we expect with N = 42 at p = 0.5
Simulate many polls of the same size from a fair process and look at the margin in votes.








Figure 2: Simulated vote margins if the true split is 50/50 (10,000 polls of size 42).





What fraction of simulated polls tie?


[1] 0.1195

title: Cheesy Snacks
section: Likelihood and Bayesian view for the Cheez-Its share
text:
Likelihood and Bayesian view for the Cheez-Its share
The binomial likelihood for p given 21 successes in 42 trials:








Figure 3: Binomial likelihood L(p) for Cheez-Its share given 21/42.





If you use a flat prior Beta(1,1), the posterior is Beta(22,22). The 95% credible interval and posterior density:








Figure 4: Posterior density for p with a Beta(1,1) prior (posterior = Beta(22,22)).





The posterior probability that Cheez-Its leads (p > 0.5) under this symmetric prior is exactly 0.5.


[1] 0.5

title: Cheesy Snacks
section: Effect size: Cohen’s h for proportions
text:
Effect size: Cohen’s h for proportions
Cohen’s h = 2asin(sqrt(p1)) − 2asin(sqrt(p2)). With p1 = p2 = 0.5, h = 0 (no effect).


[1] 0

title: Cheesy Snacks
section: Lets try a waffle chart (one square per vote)
text:
Lets try a waffle chart (one square per vote)








Figure 5: Waffle chart: one square per vote.

title: Holiday Drinks
text:
I, myself, really love eggnog (you should really try Alton Browns Aged Eggnog Recipe)



“Other” is the single most popular choice, with 19 out of 38 votes (50%)
All named drinks (non-“Other”) combined (Eggnog + Mulled Wine + Seasonal Beer) also add up to 19, so its a perfect split: Named = 19, Other = 19
Among the named drinks, Mulled Wine leads (10), followed by Eggnog (6), then Seasonal Beer (3)

This isnt a scientific poll, just a fun whiteboard vote, so think of these numbers as a snapshot of who happened to drop by the office this week. I’m going to hope that the other eggnog lovers at EPA were enjoying it on a holiday vacation somewhere.











Figure 1: Votes and shares for each holiday drink.





Now lets split the drinks into “named drinks” and “other”:





group
votes
share




Named (Eggnog + Mulled Wine + Seasonal Beer)
19
0.5


Other
19
0.5





Named drinks total: 19 (50.0%)
Other: 19 (50.0%)



If people had no real preference and were equally likely to pick any option, would we often see a split as uneven as this?

If all four options were equally popular, we’d expect about 9–10 votes per option (38 total / 4 options).
Here we saw 19 for “Other” and just 3 for “Seasonal Beer,” which is quite uneven.

We can run a simple check (called a chi‑square test) that asks: “Are these numbers more uneven than we’d expect from random chance under a 25%‑each world?” It returns a p‑value. Think of it as “how surprising is this, if there were truly no differences?”



    Chi-squared test for given probabilities

data:  results$votes
X-squared = 15.263, df = 3, p-value = 0.001605


A small p‑value means “this pattern is hard to explain by chance if all options were equally liked.” If p is below about 0.05, most folks would say “there’s a real difference here.” In this poll, the p‑value is 0.001605, which suggests the preferences are not equal across the four options.
Which options were most “surprising” relative to equal splits? Compare what we saw to what we’d expect (9.5 per option):


# A tibble: 4 × 4
  drink         observed expected standardized_difference
  <chr>            <dbl>    <dbl>                   <dbl>
1 Other               19      9.5                    3.08
2 Mulled Wine         10      9.5                    0.16
3 Eggnog               6      9.5                   -1.14
4 Seasonal Beer        3      9.5                   -2.11



Positive numbers = above the equal‑share expectation (e.g., “Other”).
Negative numbers = below the equal‑share expectation (e.g., “Seasonal Beer”).




If we repeated this whiteboard poll with a similar crowd, the exact numbers would bounce around. The dogs below show the observed share (%) for each drink. The horizontal lines show the 95% confidence interval (aka plausible ranges).








Figure 2: Share estimates with 95% confidence intervals for each category.





“Other” sits well above 25% and its plausible range stays high.
Seasonal Beer sits well below 25%.
Mulled Wine and Eggnog are in the middle, with Mulled Wine ahead of Eggnog in this sample.



Two quick, approachable views:

If all options were truly equally popular, how often would “Other” land at 19 again? The chart below simulates many “equal‑preference” polls (everyone picks randomly among 4 options) and shows where “Other” would usually fall. The dashed line shows our observed 19.









Figure 3: If all four options were equally popular (25% each), ‘Other’ would usually land near 9–10—not 19.






If people’s true preferences looked like this week’s percentages, how often would “Other” be the top choice again? Below we simulate many re‑runs using this week’s percentages as a rough guide.









Figure 4: Simulating repeat polls using this week’s percentages. How often does each drink come out on top?





In words, with this week’s pattern, “Other” would win most of the time in a same‑size rerun. Mulled Wine would sometimes take the top spot, Eggnog and Seasonal Beer would rarely do so at this size.
A handy “how many votes would need to change?” rule of thumb: to overtake the leader “Other” (19) with second‑place Mulled Wine (10), you’d need about 5 people to switch from “Other” to “Mulled Wine.” That would make “Other” 14 and “Mulled Wine” 15, flipping the lead.











Figure 5: Each square is one vote. Colors show how the 38 votes are distributed.

title: Holiday Drinks
text:
“Other” is the single most popular choice, with 19 out of 38 votes (50%)
All named drinks (non-“Other”) combined (Eggnog + Mulled Wine + Seasonal Beer) also add up to 19, so its a perfect split: Named = 19, Other = 19
Among the named drinks, Mulled Wine leads (10), followed by Eggnog (6), then Seasonal Beer (3)

This isnt a scientific poll, just a fun whiteboard vote, so think of these numbers as a snapshot of who happened to drop by the office this week. I’m going to hope that the other eggnog lovers at EPA were enjoying it on a holiday vacation somewhere.

Poll Summary
Waffles vs Pancakes (N = 27): Pancakes lead 17–10 (about 63.0% vs 37.0%).
Sweet vs Savory (N = 21): Savory leads 13–8 (about 61.9% vs 38.1%).
In this week’s group, both questions leaned roughly 60/40 toward Pancakes and toward Savory.

“p vs 50/50” is the two‑sided p‑value from an exact test; small values (like < 0.05) suggest the winner’s share is clearly above 50% in this sample.
“Leader 95% CI” is a plausible range for the leader’s true share if you repeated this kind of poll many times.
“Votes to flip winner” is the minimum number of people who’d need to switch from the leader to the trailer to change the outcome (a perfect tie isn’t possible here because both polls have an odd number of votes).

title: Breakfast Foods
text:
Both polls at a glance
Here’s a simple “leader share with 95% interval” for each question. It’s a quick way to see that both polls show a similar lean (roughly 60–65% for the winner).








Figure 5: Leader share with 95% confidence intervals for each poll.





Is there a connection between the two questions?
Short answer: we can’t directly tell, because we didn’t collect matched responses (i.e., we don’t know which Pancakes voters also picked Savory). But we can say a couple of useful things:

A quick bound, assuming the 21 Sweet/Savory voters were also among the 27 Waffles/Pancakes voters:

Pancakes + Savory overlap could be anywhere from 3 to 13 of those 21 people (14% to 62%).
Waffles + Sweet could be anywhere from 0 to 8 of those 21 people (0% to 38%).
Why these ranges? They’re just the largest and smallest overlaps that are mathematically possible given the totals (17 Pancakes, 10 Waffles; 13 Savory, 8 Sweet).

A “what‑if” check: If choices were independent (people pick Pancakes/Waffles and Sweet/Savory without a link), we’d expect roughly

Pancakes + Savory ≈ 39% of the overlapped voters (about 8 of 21)
Waffles + Sweet ≈ 14% (about 3 of 21). These sit comfortably inside the feasible ranges above.


Here’s a small visualization of those ranges, with a dot showing the “independent choices” expectation.








Figure 6: Feasible ranges if the 21 Sweet/Savory voters are a subset of the 27 Waffles/Pancakes voters. Dots show a simple ‘independent choices’ expectation.





Both polls tilt toward Pancakes and toward Savory. If many people answered both, we’d expect “Pancakes + Savory” to be more common than “Waffles + Sweet,” but the exact relationship needs matched responses to confirm. These two polls were taken in succession, not simultaneously. Different people may have voted in each question

How decisive were these results?
For each question we treat it as a coin flip (50/50) and ask: is the actual split far enough from a tie that we’d call it a real preference, or could it just be noise?
TL;DR: the loudest signal this week is Vibe — Bright & Airy beat Dim and Moody 25–7 (78%), a result that’s about as far from a coin flip as this poll gets. Meanwhile Utility was a literal coin flip: Minimalist edged Cozy by a single vote, 19–18.

Do minimalists also want function over form?
We never ask people to link their answers across questions, so we can’t say for certain who picked what on both — but we can bound it. If we assume the 28 people who weighed in on Décor are a subset of the 37 who weighed in on Utility, here’s how many “Minimalist and Functional” people could plausibly be hiding in that overlap.
Out of the 28 overlapping voters, the Minimalist + Functional crowd could be as few as 2 or as many as 19 people (7%–68%), with about 10 (37%) expected if the two preferences have nothing to do with each other. Not a tight bound, but it’s a start — we’d need paired responses to actually pin down whether “minimalist” and “functional” travel together.

None of the individual gaps clear statistical significance this week (all p > 0.28) — but the week-over-week Alignment Index takes its biggest tumble yet, down to 0.47 (cumulative, smoothed over both RTP-era polls so far: 0.89). No single matchup is a smoking gun; it looks more like everything drifted slightly in the same direction at once.
The widest gap belongs to Insect-Eaters, where HQ leaned toward Armadillo (64%) while RTP was almost perfectly torn — RTP’s raw vote on that question split exactly down the middle. It’s a good reminder that “the office average” can paper over a genuine local difference of opinion, even when it’s not big enough to call statistically significant yet.

The Alignment Index keeps climbing back toward Earth Day levels — 0.74 this week (cumulative: 0.84) — which makes it a little surprising that Breakfast produced this week’s widest office gap, 17%. The topline number recovering doesn’t mean individual questions stop splitting; look closely and this one’s a repeat of Earth Day’s Volunteering trick: the combined total for Breakfast looked almost exactly tied (28–27), but that’s because the offices actually disagree: HQ’s real preference was Breakfast Taco, while RTP’s was Bagel Sandwich.
It happened twice this week, actually — Catchphrase told the same story (HQ leaned “Bless your heart”, RTP leaned “I’m walkin’ here”, and the combined total again looked like a near-tie). That brings the running count to 4 questions since RTP joined where the topline “winner” only held up because two offices leaning in opposite directions happened to cancel each other out.

Baseball Cap isn’t just the leader, it’s the clear favorite: 63% of the vote versus Bucket Hat and Wide-brimmed Floppy Hat splitting the rest evenly. A chi-square goodness-of-fit test against “all three hats equally popular” gives p = 7.41^{-8} — nowhere close to what we’d expect if hat preference were random. This office has a type.
